notes.note: The Thracian Chersonese (modern Gallipoli peninsula, Turkey), the narrow strip of land separating the Aegean from the Hellespont; in 43 BC it lay within the theatre of operations between Dolabella (Asia) and Brutus (Macedonia).

P. Cornelius Dolabella, suffect consul of 44 BC and son-in-law of Cicero, had been declared a public enemy after murdering the Caesarian governor Trebonius in Asia; by the time of this letter he was retreating through Syria.
At the battle of Mutina (21 April 43 BC) Antony was defeated and forced to withdraw westward; Cicero is referring to news of his flight from Italy as the decisive turn of events.
This refers to Decimus Brutus Albinus (not Marcus Brutus the letter's recipient), who had been besieged in Mutina by Antony since late 44 BC and broke out after Antony's defeat.
Two major Adriatic ports in Illyricum (modern Albania) that served as Brutus's logistical bases; he had waited there for news from Italy before deciding whether to march westward or eastward.

Gaius Antonius, younger brother of Mark Antony, had been captured by Brutus in Macedonia in 43 BC; as Brutus's prisoner he was suspected of inciting the mutiny in the Fourteenth Legion from within captivity.
